title: Arm Holdings chief says AI boom is being hampered by supply constraints
excerpt: The artificial intelligence boom is being held back by what the industry cannot build rather than what customers will not buy, according to Rene Haas, chief...
content_html:
The artificial intelligence boom is being held back by what the industry cannot build rather than what customers will not buy, according to Rene Haas, chief executive of Arm Holdings PLC (NASDAQ:ARM), the chip designer.

Haas told CNBC that demand for chips, data centres, energy and skilled workers is running ahead of available capacity, creating a bottleneck he expects to persist for the next two to three years.

He was speaking in an interview at the Pennsylvania Defense and Innovation Summit, hosted by Senator Dave McCormick.

ARM designs the chip architectures that other companies license and manufacture, a position that gives it an unusually wide view of who is trying to build what.

Haas said the company expects its data-centre business to become its largest segment "very soon", overtaking the mobile phone market that built it.

That claim is the more interesting half of the interview.

ARM's designs dominate smartphones, where power efficiency is everything, and the same constraint now governs data centres, where the binding limit is increasingly electricity rather than silicon.

The supply-constrained framing also cuts against the bubble argument that has unsettled markets in recent months.

A bubble is a demand problem in which buyers pay for something they do not need.

A shortage is the opposite, and Haas is describing customers who cannot get what they are already willing to pay for.

Shares fell 5% on the session, an awkward backdrop for a chief executive describing demand as robust.

One reading is that investors are less worried about whether the demand exists than about who captures the value if the bottlenecks are physical, since power stations and skilled engineers are not things a design company can conjure.
content_text: The artificial intelligence boom is being held back by what the industry cannot build rather than what customers will not buy, according to Rene Haas, chief executive of Arm Holdings PLC (NASDAQ:ARM), the chip designer. Haas told CNBC that demand for chips, data centres, energy and skilled workers is running ahead of available capacity, creating a bottleneck he expects to persist for the next two to three years. He was speaking in an interview at the Pennsylvania Defense and Innovation Summit, hosted by Senator Dave McCormick. ARM designs the chip architectures that other companies license and manufacture, a position that gives it an unusually wide view of who is trying to build what. Haas said the company expects its data-centre business to become its largest segment "very soon", overtaking the mobile phone market that built it. That claim is the more interesting half of the interview. ARM's designs dominate smartphones, where power efficiency is everything, and the same constraint now governs data centres, where the binding limit is increasingly electricity rather than silicon. The supply-constrained framing also cuts against the bubble argument that has unsettled markets in recent months. A bubble is a demand problem in which buyers pay for something they do not need. A shortage is the opposite, and Haas is describing customers who cannot get what they are already willing to pay for. Shares fell 5% on the session, an awkward backdrop for a chief executive describing demand as robust. One reading is that investors are less worried about whether the demand exists than about who captures the value if the bottlenecks are physical, since power stations and skilled engineers are not things a design company can conjure.
content_html_zh:
芯片设计公司 Arm Holdings PLC（纳斯达克股票代码：ARM）首席执行官雷内·哈斯 (Rene Haas) 表示，人工智能热潮受到行业无法打造的产品的阻碍，而不是客户不愿购买的产品。

哈斯告诉 CNBC，对芯片、数据中心、能源和技术工人的需求超过了可用产能，这造成了他预计将在未来两到三年持续存在的瓶颈。

他是在参议员戴夫·麦考密克主持的宾夕法尼亚州国防与创新峰会上接受采访时发表上述言论的。

ARM 设计的芯片架构由其他公司授权和制造，这一地位使其能够对谁在尝试构建什么产品有异常广泛的了解。

哈斯表示，该公司预计其数据中心业务将“很快”成为其最大的业务，超过其所在的手机市场。

这个说法是采访中更有趣的一半。

ARM 的设计在智能手机中占据主导地位，其中能效至关重要，同样的约束现在也适用于数据中心，其中约束限制越来越多地是电力而不是硅。

供应受限的框架也削弱了近几个月来令市场不安的泡沫论点。

泡沫是一种需求问题，即买家为他们不需要的东西付费。

短缺则相反，哈斯描述的是那些无法获得他们已经愿意支付的东西的客户。

当日股价下跌 5%，对于一位称需求强劲的首席执行官来说，这是一个尴尬的背景。

一种解读是，投资者不太担心需求是否存在，而是担心如果瓶颈是物理性的，谁会获得价值，因为发电站和熟练的工程师不是设计公司可以召唤的东西。
